techniques.evidence_quote: The beginning of the end was a DDoS attack initiated yesterday that was accompanied by an intrusion into Code Spaces' Amazon EC2 control panel.
Code Spaces said it changed its EC2 passwords, but quickly discovered the attacker had created backup logins
we started to investigate how access had been gained and what access that person had to the data in our systems
he had removed all EBS snapshots, S3 buckets, all AMI's, some EBS instances and several machine instances
The beginning of the end was a DDoS attack initiated yesterday that was accompanied by an intrusion into Code Spaces' Amazon EC2 control panel.
he had removed all EBS snapshots, S3 buckets, all AMI's, some EBS instances and several machine instances
Code Spaces said it changed its EC2 passwords, but quickly discovered the attacker had created backup logins

attack_chain.description: Attacker likely obtained Code Spaces' AWS console credentials via phishing (noted as probable root cause in incident metadata), gaining knowledge of the EC2 control panel login.
Attacker used the stolen AWS console credentials to gain unauthorized access to Code Spaces' Amazon EC2 control panel.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The beginning of the end was a DDoS attack initiated yesterday that was accompanied by an intrusion into Code Spaces' Amazon EC2 control panel.

attack_chain.description: Attacker simultaneously launched a DDoS attack against Code Spaces as a distraction and to accompany the extortion demand, overwhelming services while the intrusion proceeded.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: The beginning of the end was a DDoS attack initiated yesterday that was accompanied by an intrusion into Code Spaces' Amazon EC2 control panel.

attack_chain.description: Attacker surveyed the AWS environment via the control panel to enumerate EC2 instances, S3 buckets, EBS volumes/snapshots, and AMIs available for destruction.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: It became clear that so far no machine access had been achieved due to the intruder not having our private keys.

attack_chain.description: Before Code Spaces could respond, the attacker created backup AWS IAM accounts and access keys to maintain persistent access, ensuring they could not be locked out.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: Code Spaces said it changed its EC2 passwords, but quickly discovered the attacker had created backup logins

attack_chain.description: When Code Spaces changed passwords in an attempt to recover control of the console, the attacker detected this and responded by escalating their destructive actions, effectively denying Code Spaces access to their own environment.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: once recovery attempts were noticed, the attacker began deleting artifacts from the panel

attack_chain.description: Attacker deleted all EBS snapshots, S3 buckets, AMIs, EBS volumes (containing database files), and EC2 machine instances, causing catastrophic and irreversible destruction of Code Spaces' entire infrastructure and data.
attack_chain.evidence_quote: he had removed all EBS snapshots, S3 buckets, all AMI's, some EBS instances and several machine instances... most of our data, backups, machine configurations and offsite backups were either partially or completely deleted.

initial_access_vector: Compromised AWS Management Console credentials (likely via phishing) used to access the EC2 control panel
persistence_mechanism: Attacker created backup IAM accounts and access keys before Code Spaces could revoke access
privilege_escalation: Attacker already had administrative-level console access; no additional privilege escalation described beyond the initial console access
